draft.introduction: The Timaeus is Plato’s cosmological dialogue, and 27d–28a poses the fundamental ontological distinction: that which always is and never becomes versus that which becomes and never truly is. This passage frames the entire creation account that follows.

translation.text: What is that which always is and has no becoming, and what is that which is always becoming and never is? The one is grasped by understanding with reason, being always the same; the other is the object of opinion with unreasoning sensation, coming to be and perishing but never truly being.
translation.original_text: τί τὸ ὂν ἀεί, γένεσιν δὲ οὐκ ἔχον, καὶ τί τὸ γιγνόμενον μὲν ἀεί, ὂν δὲ οὐδέποτε;

translator_notes.note: This passage establishes the two-world ontology that defines Platonism: the eternal realm of Forms apprehended by intellect and the temporal realm of becoming apprehended by sensation. The Neoplatonists built their entire metaphysical hierarchy on this distinction.
